item.title: Best Smart Locks for Home Security (2024 Guide)
item.description: Find the best smart locks to keep your home safe. We tested top models for security, ease of use, and smart features. Read our honest reviews and tips.
item.content:encoded:
Introduction

My neighbor Sarah texted me in a panic last Tuesday. She was 200 miles away visiting her mom when she suddenly couldn’t remember if she’d locked the front door. Instead of driving back or calling a friend, she pulled out her phone, opened an app, and locked the door from her couch. That’s the kind of everyday peace a smart lock brings — and it’s not just about convenience. Home security starts at the door, and a smart lock can be the simplest upgrade you make.

But not all smart locks are created equal. Some are built like tanks but fail when the Wi-Fi drops. Others look sleek but can be picked in seconds. After installing and testing a dozen models side by side, I’ve narrowed down the ones that genuinely improve your home security without adding headaches. In this guide, I’ll walk you through what to look for, which models earned a spot on my door, and how to install them like a pro.

What Makes a Smart Lock Secure?

A smart lock does more than unlock your door with a phone. It’s a security device first, and a gadget second. Before you buy, understand these three non-negotiables.

Encryption and Wireless Protocols

The data between your phone, the lock, and any hub must be encrypted. Look for locks that use at least AES 128-bit encryption and modern protocols like Z-Wave Plus, Zigbee, or Matter-over-Thread. Bluetooth-only locks can work for short-range access but might leave you vulnerable if the app isn’t updated. If a lock uses Wi-Fi, make sure it supports WPA3 or at least WPA2. I once tested a cheap Wi-Fi lock whose app transmitted my password in plain text — I returned it the same day.

Physical Build Quality

A smart lock replaces your existing deadbolt or attaches to it. The core mechanics still need to resist kicking, bumping, and picking. Look for locks made from zinc alloy or hardened steel, not plastic housings. Check the ANSI/BHMA grade: Grade 1 is the most secure (commercial level), Grade 2 is residential high-security, and Grade 3 is basic. I only recommend Grade 2 or higher. For example, the Schlage Encode Plus carries a Grade 1 rating, which is rare in consumer locks.

Battery Life and Backup

If the lock dies, you’re locked out — unless you have a backup. Almost all smart locks let you use a physical key as a fallback, but some keypad-only models (like the Ultraloq U-Bolt Pro) don’t. I insist on a 9V battery emergency terminal or a USB-C port for temporary power. Battery life varies wildly: the Wyze Lock Bolt runs on four AA batteries for over a year, while some Wi-Fi-heavy models need new batteries every two months. Plan accordingly and set a calendar reminder.

How We Tested and Picked the Best

I didn’t just read spec sheets. I installed each lock on a solid-core front door (my own and a neighbor’s) and lived with them for at least two weeks. Here’s what I evaluated:

  • Security: Resistance to lock bumping, picking, and brute force (I didn’t break down the door, but I checked independent test results).
  • App usability: Can my tech-averse spouse lock the door easily?
  • Smart home integration: Does it work with Alexa, Google Assistant, or HomeKit reliably?
  • Alerts and auto-lock: Does it tell me who came and went, and can it lock itself after 30 seconds?
  • Battery and backup: Real-world battery drain and what happens when it fails.

The locks below are the ones I’d bet my own house on.

Top 5 Smart Locks for Home Security

1. Best Overall: Schlage Encode Plus (Wi-Fi, Home Key)

Price: ~$300 | Grade: ANSI 1 | Battery: 4 AA, ~6 months

If you have an iPhone or Apple Watch, the Schlage Encode Plus is a game-changer. It supports Apple Home Key, which means you literally tap your device to the lock to open it — no app, no fingerprint, even if your phone battery is dead (thanks to power reserve). The lock itself is built like a fortress: solid zinc construction, a keyed cylinder for backup, and Schlage’s proven keyway that resists bumping.

I’ve used this lock for four months now. The auto-lock feature gives me a notification every time the door closes, and I can set unique codes for the dog walker that expire after an hour. My only gripe is the price, but when I compare it to a full security system, it’s a bargain.

Pros: Home Key support, Grade 1 security, works with Alexa/Google, built-in Wi-Fi (no hub).
Cons: Expensive, battery life could be better, Android users miss out on tap-to-unlock.

2. Best Budget: Wyze Lock Bolt (Bluetooth)

Price: ~$70 | Grade: Not officially rated (but feels Grade 2) | Battery: 4 AA, ~12 months

Don’t let the price fool you. The Wyze Lock Bolt is a keypad-only deadbolt replacement that skips the physical keyhole entirely — which is actually a security plus, since there’s no cylinder to pick. The keypad is durable and backlit, and you can set up to 50 unique codes. It uses Bluetooth and isn’t Wi-Fi native, but you can buy a $15 hub to enable remote access.

I put this on my garage entry door six months ago. It has survived Michigan weather, direct rain, and kids slamming it shut without a hiccup. The app is straightforward: you get logs of who unlocked the door and when. The only downside is that without the hub, you can’t control it away from home, but for a budget lock, that’s acceptable.

Pros: Punchy price, no cylinder to pick, generous code sharing, long battery life.
Cons: No physical key, requires hub for remote access, no advanced smart home triggers.

3. Best for Renters: August Wi-Fi Smart Lock (4th Gen)

Price: ~$230 | Grade: Works with existing deadbolt (retains original security) | Battery: 2 CR123, ~3 months

As a renter, you can’t always replace your deadbolt. The August lock attaches to the back of your existing deadbolt, leaving the exterior unchanged. Your landlord’s key still works, but you get keyless entry, auto-unlock when you approach, and full Wi-Fi control built in. It’s also tiny — about the size of a doorknob.

I used this in my last apartment. Installation took five minutes with a screwdriver. The auto-unlock feature uses your phone’s location so the door opens as you walk up, which feels magical. I gave my virtual keys to friends when I traveled, and I could see every time the door was unlocked. Just note: the CR123 batteries aren’t as common as AAs, so keep spares.

Pros: No permanent changes, easy install, built-in Wi-Fi, works with Alexa/Google/HomeKit.
Cons: Batteries don’t last super long, pricey for a retrofit, doesn’t improve the physical lock itself.

4. Best with Fingerprint: Ultraloq U-Bolt Pro

Price: ~$200 | Grade: Not rated (but solid zinc) | Battery: 4 AA, ~8 months

If you want the James Bond experience, the U-Bolt Pro opens six ways: fingerprint, code, smartphone app, physical key, shake-your-phone, and even an Apple Watch app. The fingerprint sensor is fast and stores up to 20 prints. It’s perfect for families — no one needs to remember a code or carry a key.

I installed this on my front door, and my kids (7 and 10) love using their fingers to get in. The lock is built tough: zinc alloy construction, an anti-peep keypad (you can type random digits before/after your code to confuse onlookers), and a backup keyway. The app isn’t as polished as Schlage’s, but the hardware makes up for it.

Pros: Fingerprint access, anti-peep keypad, works offline, versatile unlocking methods.
Cons: App feels dated, Bluetooth-only without a $50 hub, bulkier design.

5. Best for Google Home Users: Yale Assure Lock 2 (Zigbee/Thread)

Price: ~$250 (with plug-in module) | Grade: ANSI 2 | Battery: 4 AA, ~12 months

The Yale Assure Lock 2 is a modular system: you choose a wireless module (Zigbee, Z-Wave, or Matter/Thread) that slides into the top. For Google Home users who want reliable, local control without cloud dependency, the Thread module is fantastic. The lock itself has no keyhole — just a touchscreen keypad — which eliminates lock bumping entirely.

I tested the Zigbee version with a SmartThings hub for three weeks. The touchscreen wakes instantly, and you can set one-time codes from your phone. The silent operation is a nice touch if you’re coming home late and don’t want to wake anyone. If the battery dies, you can press a 9V battery to the bottom terminals to power it up temporarily.

Pros: Modular for future upgrades, reliable local smart home control, sleek look, excellent battery.
Cons: No physical key (some see this as a pro), touchscreen can be finicky in rain, not the cheapest.

Smart Lock Installation Tips

I’ve installed enough of these to know where the pain points are. Here’s what I wish I’d known on my first attempt.

DIY vs Professional Install

Most smart locks replace your existing deadbolt and use the same holes. If your door is standard (1-3/4" to 2-1/4" thick, with 2-1/8" bore hole), you can install one in about 20 minutes with a Phillips screwdriver. The instructions are clear, and many manufacturers have video walkthroughs.

If your door is older or misaligned, you may need a chisel to widen the strike plate area. That’s when you might call a locksmith — but 90% of people can handle it themselves. Pro tip: before you remove your old lock, open the door and take a photo of the latch and strike plate positions. It’ll save you a headache when aligning the new one.

A Simple Alignment Trick

If your new smart lock motor struggles to turn the deadbolt, the door alignment is off. Instead of moving the whole lock, try adjusting the strike plate on the door frame. Loosen the screws, shift it slightly, and tighten while testing the lock. Sometimes a tiny 1mm adjustment makes the difference between a grinding motor and a smooth lock.

Smart Lock Safety Features You Can’t Ignore

A smart lock is only as secure as the habits around it. Here are a few features that matter more than you think.

  • Auto-lock timer: Set it to lock 30 seconds after the door closes. On models like the Wyze Lock Bolt, this is a lifesaver if you have kids who forget.
  • Entry logs: Check the app periodically to see who opened the door and when. On my August lock, I noticed the dog walker was arriving 15 minutes late — not a security breach, but helpful info.
  • Tamper alerts: Some locks will blare a siren or send a push notification if someone tries to force the keypad or remove the cover. The Schlage Encode Plus does this well.
  • Remote access: Being able to lock/unlock from anywhere isn’t just convenient; it means you can let a trusted neighbor in during an emergency without hiding a key under the mat.

Avoid “Geofencing Only” Unlocks

Some locks offer to unlock automatically when your phone approaches. It’s convenient, but if someone steals your phone, they’re at your door. I recommend using geofencing only with two-factor authentication or combining it with a PIN/code requirement on the phone itself.

Final Thoughts

A good smart lock won’t turn your home into Fort Knox, but it will remove the most common security flaw: human forgetfulness. After months of testing, the Schlage Encode Plus is my top pick for its build quality and Home Key integration, but the Wyze Lock Bolt offers incredible value for less than $100. The best lock for you is the one you’ll actually use — and one that fits your door, your smart home ecosystem, and your budget.

Whichever you choose, take 20 minutes to install it properly, enable auto-lock, and educate everyone in the house on how to use it. When you can close your eyes at night and know your door is locked without getting out of bed, you’ll wonder why you didn’t upgrade sooner.

item.title: How to Create an Energy Efficient Home (Without Breaking the Bank)
item.description: Transform your house into an energy-saving powerhouse with practical, budget-friendly tips. Lower your utility bills, boost comfort, and reduce your carbon footprint—starting today.
item.content:encoded:
Introduction

The first month we moved into our 1960s ranch, the gas bill was so high I thought there must be a typo. The house wasn’t huge, but it leaked air like a sieve, had a furnace from the Reagan era, and the previous owner had apparently never met a window he wanted to seal. Over the next two years, my family cut our total energy use by 43% — and we did it without gutting the place or taking out a second mortgage.

You don’t need to be a building science nerd or have deep pockets to create an energy efficient home. What you do need is a clear-eyed look at where your house is wasting energy, a willingness to make small changes that stack up, and a rough plan for which upgrades give you the most bang for your buck.

In this guide, I’ll walk you through exactly what worked for us (and what I’d skip if I did it again). You’ll find practical tips ranging from completely free behavioral tweaks to mid-range investments like better insulation and smarter appliances. By the end, you’ll have a roadmap that fits your budget and your home — not a generic checklist.

Understand Your Home’s Energy Personality

Before you spend a dime, understand how your home uses energy. Every house has a unique “energy personality” shaped by its construction, climate, and the people inside.

Do a DIY energy audit (or hire a pro)

Walk through your house on a cold or windy day and look for drafts. Hold a lit incense stick near windows, electrical outlets, baseboards, and attic hatches — the smoke will dance sideways if there’s a leak. For a more high-tech approach, borrow a thermal camera from some libraries or buy an inexpensive plug-in model that attaches to your smartphone. You’ll spot missing insulation, air gaps, and even wet spots that signal poor sealing.

If DIY feels half-baked, a professional energy audit costs a few hundred dollars and often includes a blower door test that depressurizes your house and measures total air leakage. Many utility companies subsidize audits or offer them free. We paid $100 after a state rebate, and the report gave us a prioritized list of fixes — the single most useful document I’ve ever received as a homeowner.

Read your utility bills like a detective

Pull up the past 12 months of bills. Look for seasonal spikes. Do you use more natural gas in winter (likely heating) or more electricity in summer (likely air conditioning)? Does your electric bill stay oddly high even when you’re on vacation? That points to “phantom loads” — electronics and appliances draining power 24/7. Understanding your baseline helps you measure the impact of every change you make.

Start with the Free and Ridiculously Cheap Fixes

You can slash energy waste immediately without spending more than a few dollars. These moves aren’t glamorous, but they pay back instantly.

Turn down the water heater

Most water heaters come factory-set to 140°F, which is dangerously high and wastes energy. Turn it down to 120°F. You’ll still have plenty of hot water for showers, and you’ll cut water heating costs by 6 to 10 percent. Bonus: it reduces scalding risk if you have kids.

Tame the thermostat

If you have a programmable thermostat, use it. Set it to dial back 7–10°F when you’re asleep or at work. That alone can save up to 10% on heating and cooling annually. If you don’t own one, basic programmable models cost around $25. You don’t need a smart thermostat yet — though we’ll talk about those later — you just need a schedule you actually follow.

Lighten up with LEDs

If you’re still hoarding incandescent bulbs, it’s time. A single 60-watt incandescent replaced by a 9-watt LED that runs 3 hours a day saves about $7 per year on electricity. Multiply that by 40 bulbs in a typical house? You do the math. LEDs also last for a decade or more. I swapped every bulb in our house one weekend for about $80 total, and the electric bill dropped by $11 the very next month. It felt like free money.

Slay the vampires

Phantom loads — the power drawn by devices in standby mode — account for 5 to 10% of residential electricity use. Cable boxes, game consoles, computer monitors, coffee makers, and phone chargers all sip power even when “off.” Plug entertainment centers and home-office setups into smart power strips or switchable surge protectors. Flip one switch when you leave the room and watch your baseline electrical load shrink.

Adjust daily habits

  • Wash clothes in cold water (modern detergents work fine) and run full loads.
  • Air-dry clothes when weather permits — your dryer is one of the hungriest appliances.
  • In summer, close blinds on south- and west-facing windows during the day. In winter, open them to let in free solar heat.
  • Use a microwave or toaster oven for small meals instead of heating a full-size oven.

None of these require handyman skills. They just require awareness.

Seal the Envelope: Air Sealing and Insulation

If your house is drafty, everything else you do is like filling a bucket with a hole in it. Air sealing is the single most impactful thing you can do for comfort and energy bills, and it’s often a manageable DIY project.

Attack the attic first

Hot air rises, so the attic is the number-one place for energy loss. Check if you have adequate insulation — the Department of Energy recommends R-38 to R-60 for most attics in cold climates (around 12 to 20 inches of blown-in cellulose or fiberglass). If you have less, adding insulation is one of the best investments in home efficiency. We rented a blower machine from a home center for $50, bought cellulose bales, and spent a Saturday topping up our attic from R-19 to R-49. The cost was under $500, and our heating bills dropped nearly 20% the next winter. The house also felt noticeably warmer underfoot.

While you’re up there, seal any holes where pipes, wires, or recessed lights penetrate the attic floor. Use expanding foam or caulk. For recessed cans, make sure they are IC-rated (insulation contact) before burying them, or replace them with airtight LED retrofit trims.

Stop drafts at windows and doors

Replace worn weatherstripping on exterior doors — the adhesive-backed foam type is cheap and takes minutes. Install door sweeps if you see daylight under the door. On old double-hung windows, apply rope caulk or removable window film in winter. If your windows are truly awful and replacement isn’t in the budget, consider low-E storm windows, which can pay for themselves through energy savings.

Outlets and baseboards

Exterior walls are full of unsealed electrical boxes. Inexpensive foam gaskets behind outlet and switch cover plates block surprising amounts of air. Caulk along baseboards and any gap you can feel. It’s tedious but therapeutic — you’re literally plugging the leaks that make your furnace work overtime.

Upgrade to Smarter Appliances (When the Time Is Right)

Wait until an appliance dies before replacing it, unless it’s absurdly inefficient. But when you do buy, go beyond the sticker price and look at lifetime operating cost.

Refrigerators and freezers

A 20-year-old fridge can use three times more electricity than a modern ENERGY STAR model. After we replaced our 1997 side-by-side, we saved $110 a year. If you have a second fridge or freezer in the garage, consider whether you really need it — those units often run in unconditioned space and work even harder.

Heating and cooling

If your furnace or A/C is more than 15 years old, a modern high-efficiency unit (look for SEER2 ratings of 16+ for A/C, or AFUE of 90%+ for gas furnaces) can cut consumption by 20-30%. Even more important: proper sizing. Oversized equipment cycles on and off, wasting energy and wearing out sooner. A good contractor runs a Manual J load calculation; if they don’t, find someone else.

Heat pump water heaters

These units use electricity to move heat from the surrounding air into the water, rather than generating heat directly. They’re two to three times more efficient than standard electric water heaters. In our basement, it also dehumidifies the air, replacing a separate dehumidifier. Cost is higher upfront, but rebates from utilities or Inflation Reduction Act incentives can bring it down. We netted $400 off the purchase price and save about $25 a month.

Smart thermostats — but only if you use them

A Nest or Ecobee can optimize your schedule and learn when you’re away, but the real magic comes from features like geofencing (sensing when the house is empty) and remote sensors (averaging temperatures across rooms). Studies show 8-15% savings on heating and cooling, but only if you’re not overriding the program every hour. The data feedback also reveals how much you’re using, which is motivating.

Harness Free Resources: Sun, Shade, and Breezes

Your home’s orientation and landscaping can passively heat, cool, and protect it.

Passive solar in winter

If you have south-facing windows (in the Northern Hemisphere), keep them clean and unobstructed in winter. The low-angle sun streams in and warms your floors and walls. Consider thermal mass — tile, concrete, or stone — that absorbs heat during the day and radiates it at night. We removed heavy curtains from our south slider and immediately gained a couple of degrees on sunny afternoons.

Shading in summer

Deciduous trees planted on the south and west sides lose their leaves in winter, letting sun through, but provide dense shade in summer. A couple of well-placed trees can reduce air conditioning needs by up to 30%, according to the U.S. Department of Agriculture. Even awnings or exterior solar shades cut solar gain dramatically before it hits your glass.

Cross ventilation

If you live in a climate with cool evenings, open windows on opposite sides of the house to create a natural breeze. Use window fans to pull air in on the shady side and push it out on the sunny side. It’s a low-tech cooling approach that works beautifully in spring and fall, letting you keep the A/C off.

Water: The Overlooked Energy Hog

Heating water accounts for roughly 18% of home energy use. Beyond turning down the thermostat, consider:

  • Install low-flow showerheads (modern ones maintain pressure, I promise) and aerators on faucets.
  • Fix drips immediately — a hot-water leak wastes both water and the energy that heated it.
  • Insulate hot water pipes wherever accessible, especially the first six feet out of the tank.
  • For a large house with long pipe runs, a hot water recirculation pump with a timer can end the ritual of running the tap forever, saving water and energy.

We swapped our old 3.5 GPM showerheads for 1.8 GPM versions with a “pause” button for lathering. The difference in our water heating bill was measurable, and nobody complained about the pressure. The kids actually think the button is fun.

Behavioral Change That Sticks

Technology only goes so far — people run the systems. Getting the household on board is the secret sauce.

Make energy visible

Install a whole-home energy monitor like Sense or Emporia Vue. It clamps onto your electrical panel and lets you see real-time usage on your phone. Watching the wattage spike when someone turns on the toaster or dryer makes the invisible tangible. We turned it into a family game: who can get the “always on” load the lowest? My teenager became obsessed with unplugging his gaming rig.

Compete with your past self

Most utility companies offer online dashboards that compare your usage to last year or to efficient neighbors. Set a monthly target and celebrate if you beat it. Instead of “turn off the lights!” (which can feel naggy), phrase it as “let’s see if we can beat last March’s bill.” It reframes the goal from deprivation to achievement.

Cook smart

Use the right-sized burner, cover pots (they boil faster), and skip preheating for dishes that cook a long time. A toaster oven or air fryer consumes a fraction of a full oven’s energy and doesn’t heat up the kitchen — a double win in summer. My wife started baking bread in a countertop convection oven, and our A/C ran noticeably less on hot days.

The Big Picture: Renovations and New Builds

If you’re planning a major remodel or addition, you can bake efficiency into the bones of the house. Even if you’re just dreaming, knowing what’s possible helps you set a direction.

Aim for “Pretty Good House” rather than perfection

The Pretty Good House movement (coined by builders Dan Kolbert and Michael Maines) emphasizes practical, cost-effective performance over strict net-zero certification. Key moves: continuous exterior insulation (solving thermal bridging), triple-pane windows in cold climates, simple roof lines to reduce air-sealing complexity, and a small mechanical system that handles both heat and ventilation. You don’t need to chase LEED platinum to get a comfortable, low-bill home.

Ventilation matters

As you tighten the envelope, you must bring in fresh air deliberately. An Energy Recovery Ventilator (ERV) or Heat Recovery Ventilator (HRV) exhausts stale air while transferring heat and moisture, so you’re not throwing energy away. Our indoor air quality improved noticeably after air sealing, and an ERV made it consistent.

Solar panels: yes, but only after efficiency

Solar makes sense only when your house isn’t wasting energy. Reduce your load first, then size a solar array to meet the remaining need. Net metering policies vary by state, but the panels themselves are cheaper than ever. We added a 6 kW system two years after sealing and insulating, and our annual electric bill is near zero — but the efficiency work meant we could install a smaller, less expensive array. That’s the golden order: reduce, then produce.

Conclusion

Creating an energy efficient home isn’t about one heroic project or a single spendy purchase. It’s a series of small, deliberate moves that compound — just like saving money. Start with an energy audit, seal the biggest air leaks, layer in better insulation, and upgrade appliances as they fail. Let the savings from early, cheap wins fund the bigger ones.

Our house is still a 1960s ranch. It will never be a perfect passive house. But it’s comfortable, our bills are predictable and low, and we feel good about using less energy. That’s the real win: not just saving money, but taking control of your home’s impact while making it a nicer place to live.

What’s the first area you’ll tackle? Share your biggest energy frustration — or your favorite fix — in the comments. The best ideas often come from real people tinkering in their own basements.

item.title: How to Organize Your Kitchen Cabinets for Good
item.description: Learn how to organize your kitchen cabinets step by step. Declutter, categorize, and use smart storage solutions to create a functional, clutter-free kitchen.
item.content:encoded:
Introduction

You swing open the cabinet above the stove, reaching for a single can of crushed tomatoes. Instead, a tower of mismatched lids, three expired spice jars, and a stray chip clip tumble onto the counter. Sound familiar? A disorganized kitchen isn’t just an eyesore—it steals your time, raises your grocery bill (when you keep buying replacements for things you can’t find), and makes cooking feel like a chore. But a full weekend overhaul? Unrealistic for most of us.

The good news is, you don’t need a month-long decluttering marathon or a suite of custom inserts that cost a fortune. You need a clear, practical system designed for how you actually use your kitchen. Over the years, working with dozens of families as a home organizer, I’ve learned that the most successful cabinet transformations follow a repeatable formula. This guide walks you through that exact process, complete with real-life examples and actionable tips you can start this afternoon. By the time you’re done, your cabinets won’t just look better—they’ll function in a way that makes every meal prep faster and calmer.

Step 1: The Full-Empty Audit (Yes, Take Everything Out)

Before you buy a single bin, commit to taking every item out of your cabinets. All of them. I know it sounds dramatic, but I’ve never seen this step fail to reveal hidden problems. One client, Lisa, swore she had no room for her new cookware. When we emptied her lower cabinets, we discovered four identical stock pots—two still in the box—and a rice cooker that hadn’t been used since 2016. By the time we were done, she had freed up an entire shelf just by removing duplicates and unused gadgets.

As you pull items out, group them in categories on your kitchen table or counter: canned goods, baking supplies, pots and pans, food storage containers, small appliances, and so on. This visual inventory is powerful. You’ll quickly see how overrun you are in some areas (hello, 37 coffee mugs) and what’s missing in others. While the shelves are bare, wipe them down with a mild cleaner and let them dry. You’d be surprised how often a sticky residue or misplaced crumb has been lurking behind the peanut butter.

Step 2: Ruthless Decluttering—The “Maybe” Pile Has to Die

Now that everything is grouped, you need to make decisions. Go category by category, and be honest about three things:

  1. Usage: Have you used it in the past year? If not, will you realistically use it in the next six months? (Specialty holiday items are an exception, but limit those to one designated bin.)
  2. Condition: Chipped mugs, scratched non-stick pans, stained plastic containers, or gadgets with missing parts immediately go into the “toss” pile.
  3. Duplicates: Do you need five vegetable peelers? Keeps the best one or two and donate the rest.

A real example: my friend Mark kept a waffle maker that he’d used exactly twice in four years. It took up 30% of a deep cabinet. He admitted he’d rather buy fresh waffles at the farmer’s market than clean that appliance. Letting it go felt weird at first, but the reclaimed space now holds his everyday blender and toaster, which he actually uses daily. Be ruthless, but also be realistic—if you truly love your seasonal cookie cutter collection, keep it. Just give it a proper, contained home.

Donate usable items to a local shelter or thrift store, recycle what you can, and toss the rest. You’ll feel lighter already.

Step 3: Plan Your Kitchen Zones (This Changes Everything)

A well-organized kitchen isn’t about cramming things into neat rows—it’s about placing items where you naturally use them. Professional kitchen designers use a concept called “zones,” and you can apply it even in a 30-year-old rental kitchen. Think in terms of activity:

  • Cooking Zone: Stove and oven area. Store pots, pans, cooking utensils, oil, salt, pepper, and trivets here. Ideally, this is a cabinet or drawer directly next to the stove.
  • Prep Zone: The main counter where you chop and mix. Keep cutting boards, knives (in a drawer or block), mixing bowls, and your most-used spices within arm’s reach.
  • Food Storage Zone: The pantry cabinet or cabinets where you keep non-perishable goods. Keep all canned veggies together, all pastas in one spot, and baking supplies in another.
  • Dish/Cleaning Zone: Under the sink and near the dishwasher. Dish soap, scrubbers, dish towels, and cleaning sprays.
  • Container Zone: Plastic containers, lids, and reusable wraps. If possible, dedicate one drawer or a deep lower cabinet to this category; never store containers without their matching lids.

Walk through a typical cooking routine in your head. When you grab a pot, do you also reach for a colander? Store them together. When you unload the dishwasher, where do plates go? Make sure that spot is as close to the dishwasher as possible. One of the simplest fixes I gave a client with a small L-shaped kitchen was moving the silverware drawer from the far end of the kitchen to the cabinet right next to the dishwasher. It shaved 30 seconds off her unloading time—insignificant on its own, but it added up to a less frustrating daily habit.

Step 4: Choose (and Measure for) Organizers That Actually Work

This is the step where most people go wrong. They see a beautiful, Instagram-worthy lineup of clear bins and immediately order them, only to find they don’t fit their cabinets or their actual needs. Save yourself the return trip: measure the interior of each cabinet—height, width, and depth—and note any obstructions like hinges or a lazy Susan pole.

Then, shop for the problem you want to solve. Here are some of the most effective, universally useful organizers I recommend based on real kitchens:

  • Stackable wire or plastic shelves for double-decking canned goods, mugs, or small bowls. They instantly double usable vertical space.
  • Turntables (Lazy Susans) for corner cabinets, spice collections, or deep base cabinets where items get lost in the back. A 10-inch turntable can hold a dozen spice jars; a quick spin brings everything to the front.
  • Pan organizers (vertical wire racks) for baking sheets, cutting boards, and pot lids. No more clanging avalanche every time you pull out a cookie sheet.
  • Clear, medium-sized bins for grouping loose items like snack bars, gravy packets, or drink mixes. Get ones with handles for high shelves.
  • Drawer dividers for utensil drawers, but also for that dreaded “junk drawer”—give everything a compartment.
  • Over-the-door hooks or racks for the inside of cabinet doors to hold measuring spoons, pot holders, or foil and plastic wrap.

A cautionary tale: a client named Jen bought a set of “aesthetically perfect” matching white ceramic canisters for her flour, sugar, and coffee. They looked gorgeous but were too tall for her upper cabinet shelf, so she had to store them on the counter, where they collected grease and ate up prep space. She ended up donating them and using square, stackable acrylic containers that actually fit. Form must follow function.

Step 5: Arrange Items by Frequency and Heft

Now, the satisfying part—putting everything back. But don’t just shove things in. Follow a few key rules of thumb:

  • Everyday items at eye level (or just below waist level). Things you use daily—coffee mugs, breakfast bowls, go-to spices, cooking oil—should be on the most accessible shelf, typically the first or second shelf from the bottom in an upper cabinet, or the top drawer and front of a lower cabinet.
  • Occasional items higher up or in deep corners. That holiday roasting pan, the cake stand, the backup paper towels? High shelves or that awkward corner cabinet are their home. Use a sturdy step stool if needed.
  • Heavy items low. Cast iron skillets, Dutch ovens, and heavy casserole dishes belong in deep lower drawers or on the bottom shelf of a base cabinet. Your back (and the shelf) will thank you.
  • Group like with like, but sub-sort by shape. In a pantry cabinet, arrange cans in rows with labels facing forward. Square or rectangular containers make better use of space than round ones. Stack smartly—never stack heavy items on delicate ones.

A small but game-changing example: instead of nesting all your mixing bowls inside each other, store the largest on the shelf, flip the next size upside down on top (if the shape allows), or use a shelf riser to give each a dedicated spot. The goal is to eliminate the dreaded “unstack to access one bowl” maneuver.

Step 6: Label Everything—Even If You Think You’ll Remember

Labels are not just for people who love a perfectly styled pantry (though that is a nice side effect). They are a communication tool for everyone in the household. If a shelf or bin is labeled “Pasta,” your partner or kids have zero excuse for putting the rice there. It also prevents you from buying a third bottle of cumin because you couldn’t see the two you already owned tucked behind the paprika.

You don’t need a fancy label maker—masking tape and a sharpie work, or you can print simple tags. For bins on high shelves that you can’t see into, stick a label on the front facing out. For clear containers of flour, sugar, etc., label the lid or side. I once used a label to mark a high cabinet “Extra Oils & Vinegars—Check Before Shopping.” It saved my client from having six bottles of sesame oil. Little words, big impact.

Step 7: The 5-Minute Nightly Reset (And When to Re-Evaluate)

The most brilliant system fails without a maintenance habit. The good news: it doesn’t have to be a huge chore. Every evening, while you’re waiting for the kettle to boil or the dishwasher to finish, do a quick “five-minute reset.” Glance at the cabinets you opened during the day—did something get put back in the wrong spot? Are stray items accumulating on the counter that could be tucked away? Straighten a bin, reface a label, and tuck a stray packet back with its siblings.

Set a calendar reminder for a seasonal check-in (spring cleaning and pre-holiday are natural times). Food items expire, kids grow out of certain cups, and sometimes the zone you planned no longer fits your routine. That’s okay. In 20 minutes, you can reassess and tweak.

Real talk: after my son started packing his own school lunch, the snack zone I’d set on a low shelf became ground zero for chaos. We solved it by swapping that shelf with the one holding my less-used serving platters—suddenly he could reach everything, and the mess stayed contained because it was his designated space. Be open to adjusting your system as your life changes.

Organizing Is a Kind of Self-Care

It’s easy to think of cabinet organization as an aesthetic luxury, but it’s really about giving yourself the gift of an efficient, peaceful kitchen. You no longer waste money buying duplicates, you stop dreading meal prep, and you carve out a little headspace on even the busiest days. The best part? Once you follow these steps—audit, declutter, zone, contain, label, and maintain—the system tends to stick. Your future self, standing in the kitchen at 6 p.m. on a weeknight, will silently thank you when that can of tomatoes is exactly where it should be, with no avalanche in sight.

item.title: Best Home Security Systems of 2025 Compared & Reviewed
item.description: We tested 2025's top home security systems—SimpliSafe, ADT, Ring, and more—to help you protect your home affordably. Read our honest comparison.
item.content:encoded:
Introduction

Three months ago, I watched a stranger try my front door handle at 2 a.m. while my family slept. My old security camera caught it, but the grainy footage and 15-second delay made it useless. That moment pushed me to overhaul my setup—and ultimately test nearly a dozen home security systems this year. If you’re shopping in 2025, the landscape has changed dramatically. Cameras now recognize faces, sensors tell the difference between a pet and a prowler, and professional monitoring often costs less than a monthly streaming subscription.

This guide compares the six best home security systems you can buy right now. No fluff, no affiliate-driven rankings—just honest pros, cons, and real-world testing insights to help you protect what matters most.

What Makes a Great Home Security System in 2025?

Today’s systems go far beyond basic door alarms. The best ones share a few critical traits: no mandatory long-term contracts, reasonably priced professional monitoring (or free self-monitoring), easy DIY installation, and smart home integration. But the real game-changer is intelligent detection.

Modern sensors use AI to filter out false alarms—your dog won’t trigger the motion alert at 3 p.m., but an unexpected human-shaped visitor will. Glass-break sensors listen for specific sound frequencies. Environmental sensors catch water leaks, freezing pipes, or carbon monoxide early. A colleague of mine was on vacation when her basement water sensor pinged her phone. She called a neighbor, who shut off the main, saving her from a $5,000 remediation bill. That’s the kind of quiet protection worth paying for.

When comparing systems, don’t just look at the equipment price. Ask: Does professional monitoring include cellular backup? Can you self-monitor without a fee? Will the hardware still work if you cancel? We’ve kept these questions front and center.

Top 6 Home Security Systems of 2025

Here’s a quick snapshot before we break each one down:

System Best For Starting Price Monitoring Contract Smart Home
SimpliSafe No-contract flexibility $249 (5-piece) $19.99/mo None Alexa, Google
ADT Professional install & trust $599+ (custom) $29.99/mo 36 months Alexa, Google, Z-Wave
Ring Alarm Ring ecosystem users $199 (8-piece) $20/mo None Alexa
Abode Smart home tinkerers $219 (kit) $6.99/mo None Alexa, Google, HomeKit
Vivint Premium automation $599+ (custom) $29.99/mo 42+ months Alexa, Google, Z-Wave
Wyze Home Budget-conscious $98 (kit) $9.99/mo None Alexa, Google

Prices are starting MSRPs; your total depends on home size and add-ons. Monitoring plans listed are the basic professional tiers.

SimpliSafe: Best No-Contract System

Pros: Zero contract, 30-minute DIY setup, responsive app, low monitoring costs.
Cons: No HomeKit support, sensors are a bit chunky, outdoor camera requires separate purchase.

I’ve run SimpliSafe in my own home for two years, and it’s exactly what most people need. The 2025 edition added a sharp 2K outdoor camera with AI person detection. Setup is literally peel-and-stick, with the app guiding each step. Professional monitoring rings in at $19.99/month with cellular backup built in—meaning your system stays connected even if a burglar snips your internet cable. You can also self-monitor for free and just get push alerts. For renters or commitment-phobes, it’s the gold standard.

ADT: Most Trusted Name, Now Smarter

Pros: Industry giant with 150+ years of monitoring experience, professional install ensures optimal coverage, Google Nest integration.
Cons: 3-year contract, expensive equipment, early termination fees sting.

ADT has finally embraced modern tech. Their 2025 systems come with Google Nest doorbells and cameras, all manageable from one app. When an alarm triggers, ADT’s monitoring centers (six across the U.S.) respond fast—often calling you within 30 seconds. The downside? You’re locked in. If you sell your home mid-contract, you could owe the remaining balance. The pro install (about $99–$199) is thorough, but you’ll pay a premium for the brand name. Choose ADT if you want a turnkey solution and don’t mind the financial commitment.

Ring Alarm: Best for Existing Ring Camera Owners

Pros: Tight integration with Ring doorbells and cams, affordable monitoring covers cloud storage for all Ring devices, Alexa on-board.
Cons: Amazon’s data-sharing history raises privacy eyebrows, limited without cameras, no HomeKit.

If your front porch already sports a Ring doorbell, adding Ring Alarm is a natural move. The base station doubles as an Alexa speaker, and you can arm/disarm by voice. Ring Protect Pro ($20/month) includes 24/7 professional monitoring plus unlimited cloud video storage for all your Ring cameras—a strong value compared to per-camera plans. A neat hidden feature: existing Echo devices can act as glass-break sensors. Just factor in that Ring’s privacy policies allow some data sharing with law enforcement, which may matter to you.

Abode: The Smart-Home Hub That Secures

Pros: Native Apple HomeKit, deep automation engine (CUE), built-in Zigbee/Z-Wave hub, free self-monitoring.
Cons: App feels dated, no professional install option, smaller company support.

Abode is a niche darling for good reason. Its flagship hub isn’t just an alarm—it’s a full smart-home controller that can manage lights, locks, and sensors from dozens of brands. You can create automations like “when both phones leave the geofence, arm the system and lock the front door.” HomeKit Secure Video support means iCloud stores your footage end-to-end encrypted. Professional monitoring starts at a rock-bottom $6.99/month. The app’s user interface isn’t as slick as SimpliSafe’s, but if you enjoy tinkering, Abode offers unmatched flexibility.

Vivint: Premium Experience, Premium Price

Pros: White-glove professional installation, high-end equipment with active deterrents, sleek touchscreen panel.
Cons: Very expensive, lengthy contracts (often 42+ months), aggressive sales tactics.

Vivint sits at the top of the luxury security market. A typical whole-home setup can easily exceed $1,500 in equipment, plus $29.99/month monitoring. What you get: outdoor cameras that flash lights and blare sirens to scare off intruders, a dedicated tablet hub, and seamless control of thermostats, locks, and lights. The installation technician will even drill into concrete if needed. However, Vivint’s sales process often involves high-pressure pitches and financing agreements. It’s ideal for those who want a single company to handle everything and have the budget to match.

Wyze Home Monitoring: Unbelievable Value for $98

Pros: Dirt-cheap hardware, includes battery backup and cellular failover, no contract.
Cons: Build quality feels economical, app occasionally glitchy, limited integration beyond Alexa/Google.

Wyze disrupted the industry by offering a complete starter kit—hub, keypad, motion sensor, two contact sensors—for under $100. Professional monitoring adds just $9.99/month with no long-term deal. The system even boasts a battery backup and cellular SIM for outages, features typically reserved for systems costing three times as much. The sensors work reliably, though they don’t feel as sturdy. If you add Wyze cameras, you’ll want the separate Cam Plus plan for cloud recording ($1.99/month per camera). For a small apartment or a budget-conscious first-time user, Wyze is a legitimate contender.

Installation: DIY vs. Pro — Which Should You Choose?

Most modern systems (SimpliSafe, Ring, Wyze, Abode) are designed for DIY install, and you don’t need any technical skill. Sensors use strong adhesive strips; the apps provide step-by-step videos. Expect to spend 45 minutes to an hour setting up a 5–8 piece kit.

Professional installation (ADT, Vivint) costs extra but includes a security audit of your home. Technicians know how to place motion sensors to avoid false triggers and can hardwire panels neatly.

A few DIY installation tips from my own trial and error:

  • Stick door sensors in the top corner, not the center. It’s less visible and harder for someone to tamper with before entry.
  • Mount motion sensors at chest height in a corner to cover the widest area; avoid pointing them at heat vents or sunny windows.
  • Test your siren volume after setup. If you can’t hear it from the bedroom with the door closed, add an extra siren (most systems sell wireless add-ons).

Smart Home Integration: Do More With Your Security

A security system in 2025 shouldn’t exist in a silo. I’ve connected mine to my smart locks, lights, and thermostat. Here are a few practical routines you can use:

  • Auto-arm on departure: Use phone geolocation to arm the system when everyone leaves the house.
  • Entryway lights on after dark: When the front door sensor opens after sunset, turn on the porch and hallway lights.
  • Panic button shortcut: Set a bedside smart button or voice command to trigger the alarm and turn on all lights.

Abode and SimpliSafe support Alexa and Google, but Abode adds HomeKit and IFTTT, winning for advanced users. Ring naturally ties to Alexa, and you can even use Echo devices as glass-break detectors. Look for Matter support if you want future-proof compatibility—Abode is one of the few that has rolled it out so far.

Hidden Costs That Can Surprise You

A $199 kit can easily turn into $400 after add-ons and subscriptions. Keep these in mind:

  • Equipment add-ons: Extra door/window sensors ($15–$25 each), key fobs, panic buttons, indoor cameras all cost extra.
  • Professional monitoring plans: Some, like ADT and Vivint, tie you to multi-year contracts. Cancel early and you may owe 75% of the remaining balance.
  • Cloud storage for cameras: SimpliSafe includes a free plan with limited history; Wyze requires a separate plan per camera.
  • Cellular backup: Often bundled into monitoring, but not always. Confirm it’s included.
  • Equipment ownership: SimpliSafe and Ring let you reuse the hardware if you cancel monitoring. ADT may disable the panel entirely.

Money-saving tip: Buy your equipment outright instead of financing through the security company (often 20%+ APR). If you’re a renter, stick with a portable system that doesn’t require permission to drill holes.

Which System Should You Pick?

Let’s match a system to your situation:

  • Renting or plan to move within a year: Wyze (cheapest) or SimpliSafe (no contract, easy to pack).
  • Apple smart home household: Abode — native HomeKit with end-to-end encrypted video.
  • Deep into the Ring camera ecosystem: Ring Alarm — seamless and saves on cloud subscriptions.
  • Want professional install and don’t mind a contract: ADT provides peace of mind with a known brand; Vivint for the premium experience.
  • Enthusiast who loves automation: Abode with self-monitoring, paired with your own Zigbee/Z-Wave devices.

No security system makes your home impenetrable, but an effective one acts as a loud deterrent and gives you crucial evidence if something happens. Start by securing ground-floor doors and windows, add a doorbell camera, and expand outward. With the right pick, you’ll sleep more soundly—without being locked into a deal that doesn’t fit your life.

item.title: Best Smart Thermostats for Energy Savings in 2024
item.description: Cut your energy bills with a smart thermostat. Compare top energy-saving models from Nest, ecobee, Amazon, and more to help you choose the right one.
item.content:encoded:
Introduction

Heating and cooling eat up nearly half the energy in a typical home, making it the biggest utility expense for most households. As rates rise, finding ways to trim those costs without sacrificing comfort is essential. Smart thermostats do exactly that—learning your routines, sensing when you leave, and automatically adjusting schedules to curb waste. Energy Star estimates a properly used smart thermostat can cut heating and cooling bills by 8–15% annually, often saving $100 or more per year. But which model delivers the best real-world savings? This guide explains the key energy-saving features, what to look for when buying, and reviews the best smart thermostats for energy savings in 2024.

How Smart Thermostats Save Energy

Understanding the tech behind the savings helps you choose the right device.

  • Auto‑scheduling: Devices like the Nest Learning Thermostat adapt to your manual adjustments and build a schedule that matches your life. You never have to program a thing.
  • Geofencing & occupancy sensing: Using your phone’s location or built-in motion sensors, the thermostat detects an empty home and automatically sets back the temperature to save energy. As you head back, it pre‑heats or cools so you return to comfort.
  • Remote app control: Forgot to turn down the heat before a weekend away? A quick tap on your phone fixes that.
  • Energy reports: Monthly reports show consumption patterns and suggest tweaks, keeping you mindful of your habits.
  • Smart integrations: Link with door/window sensors to pause HVAC when a window is open, or use voice assistants to nudge the temperature a few degrees.

What to Look for in an Energy‑Saving Smart Thermostat

Before you buy, check these factors to ensure you get maximum savings.

  • HVAC compatibility: Most models work with low‑voltage systems (gas, electric, heat pump), but high‑voltage or proprietary setups may need special models. Always use the manufacturer’s online checker.
  • C‑wire requirement: Many smart thermostats need a common wire for constant power. Older homes often lack one. Look for models that include a C‑wire adapter or can work without one (e.g., Nest, Sensi).
  • Room sensors: Extra sensors placed in bedrooms or living areas let the thermostat focus on occupied rooms instead of wasting energy on empty ones. This is a major plus for multi‑story homes.
  • Smart home ecosystem: Pick a device that works with your existing platform—Alexa, Google Assistant, or Apple HomeKit—to enable energy‑saving routines.
  • Budget & rebates: Prices range from under $60 to over $250. Many utility companies offer $50–$100 rebates on Energy Star‑certified models, often making them nearly free.

The Best Smart Thermostats for Energy Savings in 2024

After researching dozens of models, here are our top five picks for cutting your energy bills.

1. Google Nest Learning Thermostat (4th Gen)

Best for hands‑off, automated savings

The Nest Learning Thermostat studies your temperature adjustments and builds a personalized schedule in about a week. Its Leaf icon encourages energy‑efficient temperature choices, while Eco Mode automatically setbacks the system when you’re away. Savings Finder analyzes your patterns and suggests additional tweaks. It works with most 24V systems and often doesn’t need a C‑wire. Google Home integration and the optional Nest Renew program (shifts usage to greener/cheaper times) add further benefits. Price: ~$200–$250.

Pros: Truly set‑and‑forget; elegant displays; strong energy‑saving track record. Cons: Higher upfront cost; limited Apple HomeKit support; room sensors sold separately.

2. ecobee Smart Thermostat Premium

Best for room‑sensor‑driven comfort

ecobee’s flagship includes a SmartSensor that detects occupancy and temperature in a second room. The thermostat prioritizes occupied spaces, preventing wasteful heating/cooling of empty rooms. Its eco+ software suite uses geofencing, time‑of‑use rates, and humidity data to optimize runtime. Built‑in Alexa, Siri, and a speaker make it a smart home hub. An included C‑wire adapter simplifies installation. Price: ~$250 with one sensor.

Pros: Superior room sensing; excellent cross‑platform compatibility; air quality monitor; robust energy reports. Cons: Requires C‑wire or adapter; some features may be overkill for small apartments.

3. Amazon Smart Thermostat

Best budget Alexa‑compatible option

At under $60, this Energy Star‑certified thermostat delivers core savings without the frills. It tightly integrates with Alexa to offer Hunches—automatic adjustments based on your routines, like turning down the heat when Alexa thinks you’re asleep or away. Control everything via the Alexa app or voice. It requires a C‑wire but includes an adapter. Price: $60 or less.

Pros: Incredibly affordable; strong Alexa integration; simple design; energy‑saving Hunches. Cons: No room sensors; no Google Home or HomeKit support; basic learning capability.

4. Honeywell Home T9 Smart Thermostat

Best for multi‑room sensing on a budget

The Honeywell T9 comes with one or more smart room sensors that track motion, temperature, and humidity. You can assign priority to specific rooms at different times—for instance, focus on bedrooms at night—eliminating the need to condition the entire house. Geofencing, flexible scheduling, and a clear app make it easy to manage. Works with Alexa, Google, and HomeKit. Requires a C‑wire. Price: ~$150 with one sensor.

Pros: Excellent sensor logic; intuitive scheduling; solid app and reporting; more affordable sensor expansion than ecobee. Cons: Requires C‑wire; design is utilitarian; lacks machine‑learning features.

5. Emerson Sensi Touch Smart Thermostat

Best for easy DIY install and no C‑wire headaches

The Sensi Touch shines in older homes thanks to its broad compatibility and ability to work without a C‑wire in most installations. The modern touchscreen provides 7‑day scheduling, geofencing, and usage reports. While it lacks room sensors, it still delivers reliable energy savings through flexible programming and remote access. It supports Alexa, Google, and HomeKit. Price: ~$130–$150.

Pros: Easy install without C‑wire; very broad compatibility; solid geofencing; works with all major platforms. Cons: No room sensor option; learning features are basic; design slightly bulky.

How to Maximize Your Energy Savings

Even the best thermostat needs proper setup to deliver its full potential.

  • Set realistic eco temperatures: Aim for 78°F in summer and 68°F in winter, and use a 7–10°F setback when away or asleep for at least 8 hours a day.
  • Use room sensors wisely: Place them in the rooms you actually occupy. Let the thermostat average temperatures or focus on those zones.
  • Enable geofencing or “Away” modes: This automatically slashes consumption when nobody is home.
  • Review energy reports monthly: They often reveal simple changes, like adjusting the fan runtime or a clogged filter.
  • Integrate with your smart home: Use door/window sensors to pause HVAC if a window is left open, or create a “Goodbye” routine that sets back the thermostat.
  • Seal drafts and insulate: A smart thermostat can’t overcome a leaky house. Fix the envelope first.

Conclusion

A smart thermostat is a straightforward investment that pays for itself through lower energy bills, often in under two years. The models above offer proven savings—whether you prefer the autonomous learning of the Nest, the room‑by‑room precision of the ecobee or Honeywell T9, the wallet‑friendly Alexa integration of the Amazon, or the no‑fuss installation of the Sensi. Pick the one that fits your home and habits, and don’t forget to check for local utility rebates. With a few tweaks to your settings, you’ll enjoy a more comfortable home and a noticeably smaller energy bill.

item.title: Best Cordless Vacuum Cleaners 2025: Top Picks & Buying Guide
item.description: Discover the best cordless vacuum cleaners of 2025. From pet hair to hardwood floors, we review top models for suction, battery life, and smart features.
item.content:encoded:
Introduction

For many homeowners, the chore of dragging around a heavy, corded vacuum is a thing of the past. Cordless vacuum cleaners have completely transformed the way we clean, offering lightweight convenience, powerful suction, and smart features that make housework almost effortless. As we step into 2025, the market is flooded with innovative models that push the boundaries of battery technology, filtration, and automation.

But with so many options, how do you choose the best cordless vacuum for your home? Whether you’re dealing with stubborn pet hair, delicate hardwood floors, or a mix of carpets and rugs, there’s a perfect cordless vacuum out there for you. In this guide, we’ll break down the top cordless vacuum cleaners of 2025, highlighting their key features, pros and cons, and who they’re best suited for. We’ll also walk you through the essential factors to consider before making your purchase.

After rigorous testing and research, we’ve identified five standout models that deserve a spot in your cleaning arsenal. From premium powerhouses to budget-friendly workhorses, here are our picks for the best cordless vacuums in 2025.

1. Dyson Gen5detect – Best Overall Cordless Vacuum

Dyson continues to lead the cordless revolution with its Gen5detect, a model that refines everything the brand has learned about creating a high-performance cleaning machine. Released as the successor to the critically acclaimed V15 Detect, the Gen5detect features Dyson’s most powerful digital motor yet, delivering up to 280 air watts of suction. This means it can effortlessly lift embedded dirt from carpets and capture fine dust on hard floors.

One of the standout features is the fully sealed HEPA filtration system. In 2025, air quality is more important than ever, and the Gen5detect traps 99.99% of particles as small as 0.1 microns. The machine’s Piezo sensor and laser dust detection (green light for hard floors) reveal microscopic debris you’d normally miss, making cleaning both satisfying and thorough.

The battery life has been improved, offering up to 70 minutes of runtime in eco mode, though heavy use with the motorized head cuts that to around 35–40 minutes. The LCD screen displays real-time battery life and particle count, and the bin empties hygienically with a single button. While it’s an investment, the Dyson Gen5detect is the gold standard for cordless vacuuming.

Pros: Exceptional suction; advanced dust detection; HEPA filtration; long battery life; intelligent power adjustment.
Cons: Premium price; battery is not easily removable; slightly heavier than some competitors.
Best for: Allergy sufferers, large homes, and anyone who wants the most comprehensive clean.

2. Shark Stratos Cordless with IQ Display – Best for Pet Hair

Shark has been giving Dyson a run for its money, and the Stratos Cordless with IQ Display is the brand’s best offering for 2025, especially if you share your home with furry friends. The vacuum uses Shark’s PowerFins technology, which replaces traditional bristles with flexible silicone fins that dig deep into carpets without tangling. Combined with the Odor Neutralizer cartridge, it leaves your home smelling fresh long after you’ve finished vacuuming.

The Stratos truly shines with its Anti-Hair Wrap technology. The brushroll is designed to actively separate and eject long hair and pet fur, meaning you’ll spend less time cutting away tangled messes. The IQ Display offers real-time feedback on battery life, suction mode, and maintenance alerts—helpful for first-time cordless users.

With up to 60 minutes of runtime and a removable, rechargeable battery, it’s a practical choice for whole-home cleaning. The unique MultiFLEX wand allows you to bend the stick to reach under low furniture without stooping, and it folds for compact storage. Though the dustbin is on the smaller side (0.68 quarts), the convenience and hair-handling capability make it a top pick.

Pros: Excellent hair pickup; self-cleaning brushroll; odor neutralization; flexible wand; removable battery.
Cons: Small dustbin; plastic build feels less premium; battery life drops significantly in max power mode.
Best for: Pet owners and households with long hair.

3. Tineco Pure One S15 Pro – Best Smart Cordless Vacuum

Tineco’s Pure One series has always been about intelligent cleaning, and the S15 Pro takes it to the next level. At the heart of this vacuum is the iLoop Smart Sensor that continuously monitors dust and debris levels. It automatically adjusts suction power in real time—ramping up on areas with heavy dirt and dialing back on cleaner surfaces to conserve battery. A ring of indicator lights changes from red to blue as you clean, giving you visual confirmation that the floor is truly clean.

The S15 Pro also introduces a self-cleaning station that charges the battery, stores accessories, and uses a smart sensor to detect when the base needs emptying. Once docked, the station automatically sucks out the contents of the vacuum bin into a sealed bag, reducing your exposure to allergens. This is a feature previously only found on high-end robot vacuums.

With up to 40 minutes of runtime, the S15 Pro is best suited for apartments and smaller homes, but it’s packed with premium features. The HEPA filtration ensures clean exhaust air, and the versatile attachments include a crevice tool, dusting brush, and motorized mini brush for upholstery. If you value technology and a hands-off emptying experience, the Tineco is a compelling choice.

Pros: Smart suction adjustment; automatic emptying station; compact and lightweight; HEPA filter; clear feedback.
Cons: Limited battery life; smaller bin; self-emptying bags add ongoing cost.
Best for: Tech enthusiasts, small homes, and allergy-conscious users.

4. Samsung Bespoke Jet 2025 – Best Design & Versatility

Samsung’s Bespoke Jet has always been about aesthetics, and the 2025 edition refines that with a sleek, modular design that looks like a piece of modern art when stored. But it’s not just about looks—this cordless vacuum delivers up to 210 air watts of suction and features a telescopic wand that adjusts to your height, reducing back strain during long cleaning sessions.

The Bespoke Jet comes with its own Clean Station, a dock that empties the vacuum bin into a bag with a single press—no more dust clouds. The included dual charging system lets you keep a spare battery topped up, effectively doubling your runtime to over 120 minutes if needed. This is ideal for larger homes or deep-cleaning days.

One unique aspect of the Samsung Bespoke Jet is its optional wet mop head. While the core vacuum is designed for dry debris, you can attach a specially designed mop pad and water reservoir to simultaneously clean hard floors. This 2-in-1 functionality makes it a versatile all-rounder. However, the water tank is small, so it’s best for light mopping rather than deep scrubbing.

Pros: Gorgeous design; self-emptying Clean Station; spare battery included; lightweight; wet/dry versatility.
Cons: Expensive; dust bags for Clean Station need replacement; suction slightly lower than Dyson.
Best for: Design-conscious homeowners and those who want a vacuum that doubles as a mop.

5. Bissell CrossWave Cordless Max 2025 – Best Wet/Dry Cordless Vacuum

Sometimes you need more than just dry vacuuming; spills, sticky messes, and muddy footprints call for a wet-dry solution. The Bissell CrossWave Cordless Max is the ultimate 2025 model for hard floors, capable of vacuuming and washing simultaneously. It uses a two-tank system to keep clean water and formula separate from dirty water, so you’re always mopping with fresh solution.

The CrossWave Cordless Max offers up to 30 minutes of runtime, which is enough to clean a typical kitchen, bathroom, or hallway in one go. It works on multiple surfaces, from sealed wood and tile to area rugs, automatically adjusting the brush roll speed and water flow based on what it senses. The self-cleaning cycle is a huge bonus: after use, dock it and press a button to flush the brush roll and internal tubing, making maintenance almost effortless.

What really sets the Bissell apart is its ability to pick up wet messes like cereal and milk without hesitation. It’s not a replacement for a dedicated carpet vacuum, but for homes dominated by hard surfaces, it’s a game-changer. Bissell also supports pet rescue foundations with every purchase, so you can feel good about your choice.

Pros: Vacuums and washes at the same time; easy self-cleaning; handles wet and dry messes; good value.
Cons: Not ideal for thick carpets; short battery life; tanks need frequent emptying/refilling.
Best for: Hard floor households, busy families, and quick spill cleanups.

2025 Cordless Vacuum Buying Guide: Key Features to Consider

Before you click “buy,” here are the most important factors to weigh when choosing a cordless vacuum in 2025:

Battery Life and Charging

Modern cordless vacuums typically offer between 20 and 70 minutes of runtime, but those maximum figures are almost always in low-power or eco mode. If you have a large home, look for models with removable batteries (so you can swap in a spare) or fast-charging capabilities. Some 2025 models now include docking stations that charge while also emptying the bin.

Suction Power and Motor Technology

Digital motors have become smaller and more efficient. Suction is often measured in air watts (AW) or pascals (Pa); generally, anything above 150 AW is great for carpets, while 100 AW can handle hard floors fine. Smart sensors that adjust power automatically help balance cleaning performance and battery life.

Filtration and Allergy Control

With growing awareness of indoor air quality, HEPA filtration is a must for many. Look for fully sealed systems that trap at least 99.97% of particles. If you have pets or seasonal allergies, this is non-negotiable.

Dustbin Capacity and Emptying

Cordless vacuums have smaller bins than their corded counterparts. A smart alternative is a self-emptying station (like those on the Tineco and Samsung models above), which minimizes your contact with dust. If you prefer a simpler design, a larger bin volume reduces the need for frequent trips to the trash.

Smart Features and Accessories

From LCD screens that show real-time data to laser lights that reveal hidden dust, technology is making vacuuming more efficient. Consider which attachments you’ll really use: a motorized mini brush for upholstery, a crevice tool for corners, or a soft roller for hardwood floors. A flexible wand (like Shark’s MultiFLEX) can save your back.

Weight and Maneuverability

Most cordless sticks weigh between 5 and 8 pounds, but the balance matters. Test or read reviews about how the vacuum feels in hand during extended cleaning. Swiveling heads and low-profile designs make it easier to glide under furniture.

Conclusion

The cordless vacuum market in 2025 offers something for every cleaning style and budget. If you demand the absolute best performance and are willing to pay for it, the Dyson Gen5detect remains the king. For pet owners, the Shark Stratos Cordless is a fur-fighting champion. Tech lovers will appreciate the smart sensors and auto-empty of the Tineco Pure One S15 Pro, while the Samsung Bespoke Jet blends stunning design with practical versatility. And if your home is mostly hard floors, the Bissell CrossWave Cordless Max will cut your cleaning routine in half.

No matter which you choose, upgrading to a high-quality cordless vacuum means you’ll clean more often, more efficiently, and with less hassle. Here’s to a dust-free, stress-free 2025!

item.title: Beginner’s Guide to Growing Vegetables at Home: Simple Tips for a Thriving Garden
item.description: Start your home vegetable garden with confidence! This beginner’s guide covers everything from soil prep to harvest, with easy tips for growing fresh produce at home.
item.content:encoded:
Introduction

Have you ever dreamed of stepping outside to pick fresh tomatoes, crisp lettuce, or fragrant basil for your dinner? Growing vegetables at home is easier than you might think—even if you’re a complete novice. Whether you have a spacious backyard, a sunny balcony, or just a windowsill, you can cultivate delicious, nutritious produce right at home. Not only does home gardening give you access to the freshest flavors, but it also helps you save money, reduce your environmental footprint, and find a deeply rewarding connection to your food. This beginner’s guide to growing vegetables at home will walk you through everything you need to know, from selecting your first seeds to harvesting your bounty. Let’s dig in!

Why Grow Your Own Vegetables?

Growing your own vegetables offers a host of benefits that go far beyond the dinner plate. First, the taste is unparalleled—a sun-warmed tomato straight from the vine bursts with sweetness that store-bought ones simply cannot match. You’ll also cut down on grocery bills, especially if you grow high-yield or expensive produce like herbs, salad greens, and peppers. Gardening is a sustainable choice, reducing food miles and single-use plastic packaging. On a personal level, tending plants is a proven stress-reliever; the rhythmic tasks of watering, weeding, and harvesting provide a mindful escape from screen time. It’s also a wonderful hands-on activity for kids, teaching them where food comes from and encouraging healthy eating habits. No matter your space or skill level, there’s a way to make homegrown vegetables a reality.

Choosing the Right Location

Before you buy seeds or soil, take a good look at your available space. Most vegetables need at least 6–8 hours of direct sunlight per day, so find a spot that gets plenty of sun—south- or west-facing areas are often ideal. If you have a yard, you can choose between in-ground beds, raised beds, or even vertical trellises. For those with limited outdoor space, containers, grow bags, and window boxes work wonderfully on balconies, patios, or even sunny steps. Just ensure that all containers have drainage holes to prevent waterlogged roots. Also consider proximity to a water source; lugging a heavy hose or watering can around will quickly dampen your gardening enthusiasm. A well-chosen location sets the stage for healthy, productive plants.

Essential Tools and Supplies

You don’t need a shed full of expensive gear. A few affordable basics will get you started:

  • Containers or garden beds: Pots (at least 12 inches deep for most vegetables), fabric grow bags, or a small raised bed kit.
  • High-quality potting mix or garden soil: Look for organic, well-draining mixes labeled for vegetables; avoid heavy clay or pure topsoil.
  • Seeds or seedlings: Choose varieties suited to your climate and season. Starting from seed is cheaper, but seedlings give you a head start.
  • Watering can or hose with a gentle spray nozzle: Soft watering prevents soil disturbance and damage to delicate seedlings.
  • Hand trowel and gardening gloves: A trowel handles most planting tasks, and gloves protect your hands.
  • Plant markers: Popsicle sticks or purchased labels help you remember what you planted.
  • Organic fertilizer: A balanced vegetable fertilizer or compost to feed your plants. Optional but helpful: a small shovel, pruning shears, a kneeling pad, and a sun hat.

Best Vegetables for Beginners

Some vegetables are famously forgiving and fast-growing, making them perfect for first-timers. Start with these easy-to-grow champions:

  • Lettuce and salad greens (loose-leaf, arugula, spinach): They sprout quickly and can be harvested multiple times by picking outer leaves.
  • Radishes: Ready in as little as 3–4 weeks; their quick success builds confidence.
  • Cherry tomatoes: Prolific and sweet; they thrive in pots with a support cage or stake.
  • Bush beans: No trellis needed, and they produce heavy yields over several weeks.
  • Herbs (basil, parsley, chives, mint): Compact, low-maintenance, and transformative in the kitchen.
  • Peppers (bell or chili): They love warmth and do well in containers on sunny patios.
  • Zucchini: One or two plants can feed a family, but give them ample space to sprawl. Choose vegetables you actually love to eat—watching something grow that will only go to waste is a missed opportunity.

Understanding Soil and Fertilizer

The foundation of any successful garden is the soil. Vegetables need nutrient-rich, well-draining earth with plenty of organic matter. If you’re planting in the ground, a simple soil test kit can reveal pH and nutrient levels; most vegetables prefer a slightly acidic to neutral pH (6.0–7.0). Amend garden beds with 2–3 inches of compost or well-rotted manure to improve texture and fertility. For container gardening, always use a lightweight potting mix specifically formulated for vegetables—garden soil is too dense and can compact in pots. Feed your plants regularly: a balanced organic fertilizer (like 5-5-5) or liquid fish emulsion every two to four weeks keeps nutrients steady. Remember, healthy soil equals healthy, productive plants.

Planting Your Vegetables

Timing is everything in gardening. Cool-season crops like lettuce, peas, radishes, and spinach can be planted as soon as the soil is workable in early spring or again in late summer for a fall harvest. Warm-season favorites such as tomatoes, peppers, beans, and zucchini must wait until after the danger of frost has passed—consult a local planting calendar for your region’s last frost date. You can start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before the last frost using seed trays and a sunny windowsill or grow lights, or direct sow once the soil warms up. When transplanting seedlings, handle them gently by their leaves (not the delicate stem) and water them immediately. Respect the spacing guidelines on seed packets—crowded plants compete for light, water, and nutrients, leading to weak growth and more disease.

Watering, Weeding, and Maintenance

Consistent moisture is crucial for vegetable development. A good rule of thumb is to provide about 1 inch of water per week, including rainfall. Water deeply and less frequently to encourage roots to stretch downward; quick, shallow sprinkles promote weak surface roots. Early morning is the best time to water, minimizing evaporation and allowing foliage to dry before nightfall, which reduces fungal diseases. Apply a 2–3 inch layer of organic mulch (straw, shredded leaves, or wood chips) around your plants to retain soil moisture, suppress weeds, and moderate soil temperature. Weed regularly—these uninvited guests steal water, nutrients, and sunlight. Some plants, like indeterminate tomatoes, pole beans, and cucumbers, benefit from staking, trellising, or caging to keep fruit off the ground and improve air circulation.

Dealing with Pests and Diseases Naturally

Every gardener encounters pests, but chemical intervention is rarely necessary. Prevention begins with healthy soil, proper spacing, and regular monitoring. Encourage beneficial insects like ladybugs, lacewings, and hoverflies by planting flowers such as marigolds, alyssum, and dill. Use physical barriers like floating row covers to protect young plants from cabbage moths or flea beetles. If you spot aphids, a strong spray of water can dislodge them; for larger pests like tomato hornworms, hand-pick them into a bucket of soapy water. Organic sprays, including neem oil and insecticidal soap, can control persistent problems when used as directed. For fungal diseases, avoid overhead watering and ensure good airflow. Companion planting—like growing basil near tomatoes to repel certain pests—adds another layer of natural defense.

Harvesting and Enjoying

The moment you’ve been waiting for! Harvest in the morning when plants are crisp and hydrated. Pick regularly to encourage continued production—beans, zucchini, and herbs especially benefit from frequent cutting. Leafy greens can be harvested using the “cut-and-come-again” method: snip the outer leaves and let the inner core keep growing. Most vegetables taste best when young and tender; don’t wait until they become oversized and woody. Use your homegrown produce in simple dishes that let the freshness shine: a tomato-basil salad, a crisp radish toast, or a handful of greens in a smoothie. Remember, gardening is a constant learning journey. Celebrate each success, and don’t be discouraged by a few setbacks—they are the best teachers for next season.

Common Mistakes to Avoid

Even the most seasoned gardeners have made these missteps—learn from them early!

  • Over- or under-watering: Stick your finger an inch into the soil; if it’s dry at your fingertip, it’s time to water.
  • Planting without sunlight: 6+ hours of direct sun is non-negotiable for most veggies.
  • Ignoring soil health: Starved soil yields starved plants; feed your soil first.
  • Overcrowding: More plants in a small space does not equal more harvest—it invites disease.
  • Forgetting to harden off: Seedlings started indoors need a gradual introduction to outdoor conditions over a week.
  • Giving up too soon: Every gardener loses plants. It’s a natural part of learning, not a failure.

Next Steps

Start small—choose two or three of the beginner-friendly vegetables above, grab a container or clear a small patch, and plant your first seeds. Keep a simple garden journal to track what you planted, when, and how it performed, so you can refine your approach each year. There is a wealth of online resources, local gardening groups, and extension services ready to help. Before you know it, you’ll be harvesting homegrown meals and enjoying the profound satisfaction of having grown them yourself. So go ahead, get your hands dirty, and taste the difference.

item.title: How to Paint a Room Like a Professional: A Step-by-Step Guide
item.description: Learn how to paint a room like a pro with our step-by-step guide. From prep to cleanup, get insider techniques for a flawless, long-lasting finish.
item.content:encoded:
Introduction
